title: Oracle shares slide as hefty AI spending, debt plans spook investors
content: Shares of Oracle Corporation tumbled despite the company reporting stronger-than-expected quarterly results, as investors focused on the mounting costs of its AI expansion rather than its earnings beat.For the fiscal fourth quarter, Oracle posted revenue of $19.18 billion, up 21% year-on-year and ahead of analysts' estimates of $19.1 billion. Adjusted earnings came in at $2.03 per share, also surpassing expectations of $1.96, according to LSEG.However, the company's ambitious AI infrastructure push continues to worry investors. Oracle plans to spend around $70 billion on capital expenditure this fiscal year as it ramps up data-centre capacity to serve AI customers, including OpenAI.To finance this expansion, the company said it will raise $40 billion through debt and equity, including a previously announced $20 billion share sale. This comes after Oracle raised $43 billion in debt and $5 billion in equity in the previous fiscal year.The scale of spending has intensified concerns over profitability and cash generation. Oracle's free cash flow remained deeply negative at $23.7 billion, raising questions about how quickly its AI investments will translate into meaningful returns.Analysts have also flagged risks around funding requirements, margin pressure and the company's ability to keep pace with cloud giants such as Microsoft and Amazon, reported Reuters.In essence, while Oracle's latest results impressed, the market reaction reflected investor anxiety over whether its costly AI bet will eventually deliver sufficient profits to justify the massive outlay.

title: Deloitte India and SAP launch AI-led delivery centre in Bengaluru
content:
Deloitte India announced the inauguration of its dedicated SAP Delivery Centre in Bengaluru. The strategic AI-led innovation and delivery hub is designed to support SAP’s transformation priorities and help enterprises accelerate cloud adoption, intelligent automation and AI-powered business transformation.

This centre marks one of Deloitte’s first strategic SAP-focused delivery centres in India, established to enable large-scale enterprise transformation through innovation-led delivery, advanced engineering capabilities and specialised AI talent.

The Deloitte–SAP Delivery Centre will support SAP and joint customers across the end-to-end transformation lifecycle, spanning innovation, architecture, engineering, implementation and managed evolution of enterprise solutions. It will focus on helping organisations modernise legacy systems, accelerate adoption of SAP cloud solutions, unlock business value through AI and build intelligent, future-ready operating models.

Talent development

The centre will be supported by an ecosystem of more than 1,000 professionals across India and is expected to further expand through the addition of approximately 300 to 500 specialised AI professionals over the next 12 to 24 months. Talent development efforts will focus on high-demand areas including generative AI, agentic AI, machine learning, cloud architecture, data engineering and intelligent automation.

Sathish Gopalaiah, President, Consulting Businesses, Deloitte South Asia, said, “The Deloitte–SAP Delivery Centre underscores our shared commitment to driving innovation through AI, cloud and advanced technologies. As organisations accelerate digital transformation, this centre will serve as a catalyst for developing next-generation solutions, enabling SAP and our joint clients to harness emerging technologies, scale innovation and deliver differentiated, technology-led outcomes globally.”

A key component of the centre’s vision is the establishment of an AI prototyping lab designed to foster experimentation and accelerate innovation. The initiative aims to make AI innovation more accessible by enabling individuals across technical and business functions to transform ideas into practical solutions through access to tools, mentorship and collaborative development environments.

The centre will also support the development of scalable assets, frameworks and accelerators that help organisations simplify complex transformation journeys, improve operational efficiency and enhance business outcomes through intelligent technologies.

Published on June 11, 2026

title: India can take the lead in applying AI in industries: SAP CEO Christian Klein
content: Artificial Intelligence models are becoming commoditised, so the bigger opportunity lies in applying AI in industries — manufacturing, finance, life sciences, supply chains and professional services. “That’s where countries like India can become frontrunners,” SAP chief executive Christian Klein told Surabhi Agarwal and Shristi Achar in a video interview.The risk to India or the global IT and software services companies is only “if you are not transforming your workforce” to make it AI aligned, said Klein, 46, during his annual visit to India. Edited excerpts:Of course, there are geopolitical tensions, including the West Asia conflict, and risks around inflation and supply chain disruptions. But we have not seen a downward trend in IT spending. In fact, we continue to see a strong upward trend because companies increasingly view AI and IT as part of the solution to these challenges. Earlier, the focus was simply on investing in AI. Now the focus is on productivity gains and business outcomes.At some point, the business case has to stack up. If inflation remains high, companies need to find productivity improvements somewhere in their operations, and AI can become a very strong lever for that.It’s very important for us that customers have a choice and that we can run our full portfolio in India and out of India. When it comes to large language models, we give customers a choice. We are offering models from India and also the latest models coming from the US. Customers can move from model to model. There is no lock-in.That independence is very important because customers do not want to become too dependent on a certain state or take on higher geopolitical risks. At the same time, I would not focus too much on trying to replicate existing LLMs. India already has extremely talented startups and engineers building AI solutions. I already see LLMs becoming commoditised. There will not be only one or two dominant models.The bigger opportunity is applying AI to industries —manufacturing, finance, life sciences, supply chains and professional services. That is where countries like India can become frontrunners. The only risk as a software company or as a services company is if you are not transforming your workforce.India has all the ingredients required to win in AI. First, the talent pool is extraordinary. We find engineers and developers here who we do not find anywhere else in the world. The quality of technical talent and universities is extremely strong.Second, India is investing heavily in AI infrastructure. Companies like TCS , Adani and Airtel are building the infrastructure that AI ecosystems need.Third, you have a government that is strongly probusiness and focused on making India an even better place to invest in.I recently met Prime Minister (Narendra) Modi in Sweden, and we discussed many of these initiatives. And globally, many companies increasingly see India as a trusted investment destination amid geopolitical tensions between China and the US. When I speak with European CEOs, many of them want to increase investments in India because of the resilience, stability and long-term opportunity they see here.I remain very optimistic about the Indian IT ecosystem. Yes, there will be short-term disruptions. Some tasks that consultants perform today, especially around ERP (enterprise resource planning) migration or repetitive implementation work, will increasingly be automated by AI tools.But at the same time, entirely new categories of work are emerging. AI agents still need to be implemented, trained, governed and adapted to complex enterprise environments. They need to understand business processes, data structures, regulations and customer-specific system landscapes.We need our Indian partners to enable the agents to run in different system landscapes. We also need our Indian partners because they have a very good understanding of business processes and data.We are hiring aggressively in India, particularly for full-stack AI developers, forward-deployed engineers, data engineers and data scientists. As we build AI agents for enterprise use cases, we need engineers who can work directly with customers and deploy these systems across complex business environments.We also need experts who can work on data layouts, AI foundations and training models on enterprise data. India is central to that strategy. We are investing heavily in partnerships with top universities in cities like Bengaluru and Delhi. We are not just recruiting students after graduation — we are engaging with them while they are still studying, training them on AI development and involving them in projects early on.India is also the only country outside Germany where SAP has introduced its vocational student programme, where students alternate between working at SAP and attending university. That combination of practical experience and education has been very successful for us.
description: SAP CEO Christian Klein believes India can lead in applying AI to industries like manufacturing and finance, rather than just replicating large language models. He emphasised workforce transformation and sees India's talent, infrastructure investment, and government support as key to its continued tech powerhouse status amidst global AI shifts.
text: India can take the lead in applying AI in industries: SAP CEO Christian Klein SAP CEO Christian Klein believes India can lead in applying AI to industries like manufacturing and finance, rather than just replicating large language models. He emphasised workforce transformation and sees India's talent, infrastructure investment, and government support as key to its continued tech powerhouse status amidst global AI shifts. Artificial Intelligence models are becoming commoditised, so the bigger opportunity lies in applying AI in industries — manufacturing, finance, life sciences, supply chains and professional services. “That’s where countries like India can become frontrunners,” SAP chief executive Christian Klein told Surabhi Agarwal and Shristi Achar in a video interview.The risk to India or the global IT and software services companies is only “if you are not transforming your workforce” to make it AI aligned, said Klein, 46, during his annual visit to India. Edited excerpts:Of course, there are geopolitical tensions, including the West Asia conflict, and risks around inflation and supply chain disruptions. But we have not seen a downward trend in IT spending. In fact, we continue to see a strong upward trend because companies increasingly view AI and IT as part of the solution to these challenges. Earlier, the focus was simply on investing in AI. Now the focus is on productivity gains and business outcomes.At some point, the business case has to stack up. If inflation remains high, companies need to find productivity improvements somewhere in their operations, and AI can become a very strong lever for that.It’s very important for us that customers have a choice and that we can run our full portfolio in India and out of India. When it comes to large language models, we give customers a choice. We are offering models from India and also the latest models coming from the US. Customers can move from model to model. There is no lock-in.That independence is very important because customers do not want to become too dependent on a certain state or take on higher geopolitical risks. At the same time, I would not focus too much on trying to replicate existing LLMs. India already has extremely talented startups and engineers building AI solutions. I already see LLMs becoming commoditised. There will not be only one or two dominant models.The bigger opportunity is applying AI to industries —manufacturing, finance, life sciences, supply chains and professional services. That is where countries like India can become frontrunners. The only risk as a software company or as a services company is if you are not transforming your workforce.India has all the ingredients required to win in AI. First, the talent pool is extraordinary. We find engineers and developers here who we do not find anywhere else in the world. The quality of technical talent and universities is extremely strong.Second, India is investing heavily in AI infrastructure. Companies like TCS , Adani and Airtel are building the infrastructure that AI ecosystems need.Third, you have a government that is strongly probusiness and focused on making India an even better place to invest in.I recently met Prime Minister (Narendra) Modi in Sweden, and we discussed many of these initiatives. And globally, many companies increasingly see India as a trusted investment destination amid geopolitical tensions between China and the US. When I speak with European CEOs, many of them want to increase investments in India because of the resilience, stability and long-term opportunity they see here.I remain very optimistic about the Indian IT ecosystem. Yes, there will be short-term disruptions. Some tasks that consultants perform today, especially around ERP (enterprise resource planning) migration or repetitive implementation work, will increasingly be automated by AI tools.But at the same time, entirely new categories of work are emerging. AI agents still need to be implemented, trained, governed and adapted to complex enterprise environments. They need to understand business processes, data structures, regulations and customer-specific system landscapes.We need our Indian partners to enable the agents to run in different system landscapes. We also need our Indian partners because they have a very good understanding of business processes and data.We are hiring aggressively in India, particularly for full-stack AI developers, forward-deployed engineers, data engineers and data scientists. As we build AI agents for enterprise use cases, we need engineers who can work directly with customers and deploy these systems across complex business environments.We also need experts who can work on data layouts, AI foundations and training models on enterprise data. India is central to that strategy. We are investing heavily in partnerships with top universities in cities like Bengaluru and Delhi. We are not just recruiting students after graduation — we are engaging with them while they are still studying, training them on AI development and involving them in projects early on.India is also the only country outside Germany where SAP has introduced its vocational student programme, where students alternate between working at SAP and attending university. That combination of practical experience and education has been very successful for us.

title: SAP Labs India Unveils 2026 Startup Studio Cohort Focused on Enterprise AI and Deep
content:
BusinessWire India

Advertisement

Bangalore (Karnataka) [India], June 3: SAP Labs India today announced the launch of SAP Startup Studio Cohort 2026, marking a decade of its flagship open innovation accelerator program focused on enabling startups to co-innovate, build enterprise-ready solutions, and scale through SAP's global ecosystem. The six-month accelerator brings together high-potential startups across enterprise AI, agentic systems, quantum-safe security, robotics, cloud infrastructure, industrial intelligence, and sustainable manufacturing, with a strong focus on helping startups 'build with SAP' through deep collaboration with SAP's product and engineering teams. Designed for early and growth-stage startups, the program provides access to SAP leaders, enterprise customers, partners, and global commercialization opportunities. The startups shortlisted for SAP Startup Studio Cohort 2026 include ANSCER Robotics, Oorja, Vizionsys Technologies, Astrikos, Drishya AI, Endee Labs, Grevoro, Quanfluence, QNu Labs, FUSKI AI, Ardent, Neoflo, SaarthiOS, Perceptory AI Labs, SecuAI, and Pulse Energy.

Advertisement

The cohort was selected through a multi-stage evaluation process including pitch sessions with SAP leaders and domain experts, focusing on enterprise relevance, innovation depth, scalability, and co-building potential within SAP's ecosystem.

Advertisement

Speaking on the occasion, Sindhu Gangadharan, MD, SAP Labs India and President, Indo-German Chamber of Commerce, said, "At SAP, we believe meaningful innovation is driven through strong ecosystems, deep collaboration, and the ability to co-create with startups addressing real business and industry challenges. As we mark a decade of SAP Startup Studio, we are proud to continue fostering a platform that enables startups to work closely with SAP's product, engineering, customer, and partner ecosystem to build scalable, enterprise-ready innovations for global customers.

The 2026 cohort brings together startups working across areas such as enterprise AI, agentic systems, robotics, industrial intelligence, energy innovation, and quantum-safe security -- technologies that are expected to play an increasingly important role in shaping the future of enterprises and industries worldwide. We look forward to supporting these startups through mentorship, collaboration, and access to SAP's global innovation ecosystem."

Advertisement

SAP Startup Studio is designed to provide startups with a structured innovation journey spanning co-creation, validation, commercialization, and scale. Through the program, startups gain access to SAP leadership, domain experts, enterprise customers, and SAP's global partner ecosystem, along with opportunities for go-to-market collaboration and enterprise adoption.

The key focus areas for the 2026 Cohort include enterprise AI and agentic systems, quantum computing and secure enterprise technologies, cloud and platform innovation, industry transformation, and emerging technologies such as robotics, distributed cloud, and enterprise automation. Through Startup Studio, SAP continues to strengthen India's position as a global innovation hub while enabling startups to scale enterprise impact through collaboration and co-creation. In the past, QpiAI, Cloudworx, Yellow.ai, Skillate, AgriPilot.ai, MedySeva, Intelekt AI, Prodoc AI, and Avysh, amongst many others, were part of the SAP Startup Studio cohort.

The program also reinforces SAP's role as an ecosystem orchestrator, bringing together startups, SAP product teams, customers, and partners into a unified innovation platform that enables a continuous co-creation, validation, commercialization, and scale loop to drive the next wave of enterprise transformation globally.

(ADVERTORIAL DISCLAIMER: The above press release has been provided by BusinessWire India. ANI will not be responsible in any way for the content of the same)

(This content is sourced from a syndicated feed and is published as received. The Tribune assumes no responsibility or liability for its accuracy, completeness, or content.)

title: Tata Technologies secures SAP PartnerEdge Sell authorization in India and US
content:
Tata Technologies has secured the SAP PartnerEdge Sell authorization across India and the United States, strengthening its strategic role within the SAP ecosystem. Announced on June 1, 2026, this authorization enables the company to transition from a services-led model to a solution-led, outcome-driven approach, enhancing its ability to deliver end-to-end enterprise and AI-led transformation capabilities.

Strategic Shift to Solution-Led Model

The authorization marks a significant evolution in Tata Technologies' go-to-market strategy. Under this new model, the company will lead customer engagements across the full lifecycle, including solution advisory, cloud ERP transformation, solution design, and value realization. This shift is designed to help enterprises accelerate SAP Cloud ERP adoption and modernize their digital core for real-time, data-driven decision-making.

Enhancing Enterprise Capabilities

By leveraging its deep expertise in manufacturing, Tata Technologies aims to support global OEMs and enterprises in reducing implementation risks. The company will serve as a single partner across the SAP journey, ensuring seamless and outcome-focused transformation. Additionally, Tata Technologies will help organizations harness SAP Business AI to embed intelligent capabilities into enterprise workflows, utilizing Joule, SAP's AI-enabled business assistant, to enhance productivity and automate processes.

Alignment with SAP's Vision

The integration with the SAP Business Technology Platform allows enterprises to extend and innovate across their SAP landscapes while maintaining a resilient digital core. Aligned with SAP's Clean Core strategy, Tata Technologies supports enterprises in adopting innovation without disrupting legacy systems, ensuring standardized and upgrade-stable environments.

Parameter Details Authorization SAP PartnerEdge Sell Authorization Geography India and United States Focus Area Enterprise and AI-led Transformation Key Capability SAP Cloud ERP and SAP Business AI

Mr. Warren Harris, MD & CEO, Tata Technologies, stated that expanding the partnership enables the company to deliver transformative solutions that help customers reimagine their businesses in an increasingly digital world. This development reinforces Tata Technologies' position as a trusted global partner for enterprise and AI-led transformation.

title: ‘SAP to hire more AI talent in India’: Philipp Herzig, CTO, SAP
content:
In an exclusive interview, SAP Chief Technology Officer Philipp Herzig addresses global job displacement anxieties, defends the enterprise readiness of AI-driven ERP architectures over raw market hype, and highlights India’s position as a vital testbed and talent hub for early agentic deployment. There is a growing concern globally that AI will replace jobs. What is your view?
I look at agentic coding as canary in a coal mine for what will also happen with many other professions. Initially, people predicted that developers would disappear. Th... [3781 chars]
description: In an exclusive interview, SAP Chief Technology Officer Philipp Herzig addresses global job displacement anxieties, defends the enterprise readiness of AI-driven ERP architectures over raw market hype, and highlights India’s position as a vital testbed and talent hub for early agentic deployment.

title: From SaaS to silicon: How Zoho and a team of fresh engineering grads built an indigenous enterprise server
content:
When Mangesh Sadafale, the Head of Hardware Development at Zoho, went to Sikkim on a family vacation a few years ago, he could not see, or rather he could not cross the iconic Nathu La pass due to bad weather. Years later, he would find himself staring down a different kind of impassable terrain. The road to designing an enterprise-grade, homegrown data centre server from scratch seemed as arduous. Turns out, calling it “Nathu La” was the easiest job.

Read Full Story

“I proposed, can we give it the Nathu La pass name? The team said yes,” Sadafale tells India Today Tech. “Then we decided, when ‘La’ and ‘Pass’ mean the same thing, why keep ‘Pass’ there? We will be Indian and keep whatever our local people use. We removed the ‘Pass’.” The rest, as they say, is history.

In a country whose tech narrative has been almost exclusively written in lines of code, a software company may have just pulled off the hardest trick in deep-tech hardware. But the audacity doesn’t end there. The team that has designed the Nathu La server isn’t a room full of Silicon Valley veterans. It is a crew of fresh engineering graduates from the quiet, central city of Nagpur.

Why Nathu La

To be fair, Nathu La wasn’t built in a day. Research and development began five years ago when sanctions on chips and now, on advanced AI models like Claude Fable 5 and Mythos, were few and far between. In a sense, it was foresight. What’s wild is that a SaaS startup thought about it first. Not some heavyweight like TCS or Infosys.

“Inference cost is now a line item and not a rounding error,” explains Ramprakash Ramamoorthy, Director of AI Research at Zoho. “Typically, if you look at a software company's balance sheet, the bigger expense is going to be people. The second thing is going to be operations like travel, office, and all of that. The third thing is your server cost which is your inference cost. Not just AI inference, all inference. But now it (inference cost) is moving up, and eventually, I think next year it will become the first big line item for a software company's expenses.” Ramprakash Ramamoorthy is the Director of AI Research at Zoho Corporation

Over the past six months alone, the tech industry has watched global component pricing spike to unprecedented heights. Generic servers have seen their market prices surge by four times compared to December 2025, Ramamoorthy reveals. Securing these components, too, is an uphill task as demand is far exceeding the supply. Procurement delays can stretch into multiple quarters. Most big players are relying on public cloud companies or third-party Large Language Model (LLM) providers, handing over massive chunks of their revenue to keep the lights on. When those infrastructure costs go up, the surplus gets pushed downstream. It was clear from the start that Zoho would not pass the burden to its customers.

advertisement

“Renting (hardware) forever stops making sense because the competition is getting cutthroat, and then we are primarily building on top of the infrastructure that we have underneath,” says Ramamoorthy. "So, if compute becomes foundational, we should own it. We want to be a predictable and a boring company to do business with. That is one of the reasons we invest a lot in the foundation.”

Underneath all the chaos and uncertainty, lurks another big issue, that of national significance.

“If we look at the top three imports what India has done in the last couple of years, electronics and semiconductors stay at the third position, very close to gold with a narrow margin. Now, in compute electronics, for India, electronics is the third trade deficit, whereas if we compare it with China, electronics is the top number one contributor to their trade surplus,” Sadafale points out.

In the last financial year alone, India imported more than $13 billion worth of complete compute systems. In stark contrast, during that exact same timeframe, China exported more than $150 billion of compute hardware. When Indian enterprises purchase servers today, the vast majority of those funds do not stay within the domestic ecosystem. Instead, the capital flows directly to the countries and multinational corporations that own the core intellectual property.

advertisement

“The country which owns the IP, owns the design, gets the bigger section of the payment, and the country which manufactures it gets the relatively smaller section of the payment,” Sadafale explains. “Now, when we look at it from an India perspective, the IP creation of critical infrastructure like server networks is very important. If we create IP, if we create a design, then only we will be able to sustain that cash flow or stop the outflow of money from India.”

Sadafale cites the example of manufacturing, or rather, assembling of smartphones in India to make his point. “India doesn't have a solid mobile brand which owns the solid mobile IP and mobile design which can be manufactured in India and exported, so India is paying royalty to some other company outside, and the cash flow is going out of India. Deep tech knowledge cannot be licensed. It has to be homegrown. To maximise the value addition in India, the design has to be made in India,” he says candidly.

advertisement

Inside Nathu La

When Zoho started its hardware initiative, it skipped over the crowded, hyper-competitive tech ecosystems of Bengaluru, Chennai, and Delhi. Instead, it established the hardware team in Nagpur, with the reasoning that it was located in "central" India.

However, Zoho hit an immediate roadblock. In India, the specialised talent pool capable of executing advanced system design or Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) engineering is notoriously small, heavily concentrated in specific corporate corridors, and highly resistant to relocating to a Tier-2 city.

“The only available talent pool was freshers in and around local colleges,” Sadafale says. “But the freshers, as everyone knows, they are more interested in software or AI. No one is interested in hardware. By default, hardware is hard. No one wants to work on a hard problem. And hardware is a hard problem. Over and above, in the last few years, AI is also creating trouble. It is spoiling the creativity of students.

To counter this, Zoho engineered the SETU program (Student Engagement for Transformative Upskilling), stepping into local government engineering colleges to look for raw, unfiltered curiosity.

advertisement

“We don't care about CGPA or marks. We just care about curiosity and ability to learn,” Sadafale emphasises. “In SETU, we start interaction with students in the fifth semester itself. We give them a basic problem statement in hardware design. It can be mechanical, electronics, or firmware. As the students move from the sixth and seventh semester, the problem statements become more complicated. And at the end of the semester, based on how the students have performed, we offer them an internship in the eighth semester.”

To date, the SETU program has trained over 300 students. It aligns closely with the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology's (MeitY) skill development initiatives for the Electronic System Design and Manufacturing (ESDM) sector.

But more importantly, an astonishing 90 percent of the engineering team responsible for creating the Nathu La server platform is composed of these local college graduates who joined the firm over the last five years. Excluding Sadafale's own 25 years of industry expertise, the average professional experience across the entire Nagpur hardware office sits at just three to four years.

Throwing early-career engineers directly into the deep end of advanced enterprise server development may sound like a recipe for disaster, but Sadafale believes their lack of institutional bias is a major competitive advantage.

“Freshers can do magic because they don't know what is complex and what is simple,” he notes. “So, (when) you throw the problem at them, they will figure out the solution. You just have to protect them and help them in a way that it nurtures them, and it works out. It's great. It just works.”

Nathu La is a living example of it. A high-performance enterprise server requires three distinct engineering areas to operate in perfect harmony: mechanical design for structural integrity and cooling, electronics design for high-speed signal distribution, and firmware to bridge the physical circuitry with the operating system. The Nagpur team systematically tackled all three.

At its core, the Nathu La motherboard is built around the Open Compute 'M-DNO' form factor, a highly modular architecture that allows the system to be rapidly reconfigured for vastly different enterprise data centre workloads. Rather than deploying rigid, generic servers that run at subpar efficiency, Zoho can alter the underlying module configurations to precisely match the software application running on top of them.

“Nathu La is a modular design which is workload-optimised,” Sadafale explains. “In Zoho Mail, we normally send text messages. Text is relatively small data information saved in storage to retrieve it when we want to see the mail again. Whereas in a Meeting, it is audio-video information, which is relatively much higher data. It has to be processed more because we are doing screen sharing, recording, background removal, and multiple people are changing their configuration. Meeting needs a different type of compute, a different type of server, whereas Mail needs a different type of compute and server.” Mangesh Sadafale, the Head of Hardware Development at Zoho

By tailoring the server architecture to the specific data profile of the application, Nathu La can achieve an impressive 12 to 18 percent lower power consumption profile alongside a remarkable 20 to 30 percent reduction in total cost of ownership (TCO), Zoho claims. The platform boasts five distinct patents, the bigger pieces going into cooling.

“Thermal is the number one concern today even if I take an air-cooled system like Nathu La,” says Sadafale. “The future systems will be liquid cooled. But in air-cooled also, our patents, the majority of them are for cooling. How efficiently you cool the systems, what are the small things, how the air flows, that is the actual patent. People will say, ‘What is the big deal?’ But that is a big deal.”

Beyond the physical steel and circuitry lies the critical layer of firmware. Nathu La eliminates the opaque “black box” code supplied by traditional overseas hardware vendors, replacing it with a completely auditable, transparent “white box” software stack based on open-source standards like EDK2 for the BIOS and OpenBMC for the Baseboard Management Controller.

The BMC acts as the operational nervous system of a modern data centre, allowing a single operator to monitor, diagnostic test, and maintain thousands of separate server stacks from a centralised console. By modifying open-source code for their specific needs, Zoho’s infrastructure engineers can know exactly what is happening inside every single data path.

“We took open-source code and then modified it for our needs,” Sadafale explains. “So, we know each and every line of the code that has gone in. We know what signal is going here and there. There is hardware security we have added in so that even if someone takes the server outside (the country) and tries to hack something, that will not happen.”

Beyond Nathu La

The rollout of the Nathu La server platform coincides with a broader conversation regarding the sustainable integration of AI within the enterprise ecosystem. We know – and we have seen – that AI tools like Claude Code can upend the SaaS industry. Whether it can bring the SaaSpocalypse, that remains debatable. But as the initial wave of excitement settles, the market is beginning to demand tangible proof of value, that which OpenAI, Anthropic and even Google, have promised.

“The whole craze for all these memory prices going crazy, all of this is due to the AI mad rush,” Ramamoorthy notes. “But the good thing is I see some of it slowing down, at least in the last couple of weeks. We're hearing a lot more conversations about return on token investment – ‘I spent so much, what is the tangible output that I have received?’”

Across its corporate legal workflows, sales optimisation pipelines, and customer support channels, Zoho is seeing a steady, incremental 20 to 25 percent improvement in overall employee productivity using AI.

“If a human support engineer is solving 10 tickets a day, they will be able to solve 12 tickets a day,” Ramamoorthy states plainly. “And that is the incremental benefit that we have seen so far. The reality is coming back to the table. I hope that contains the mad rush of throwing crazy compute at internet-scale data. It (AI) definitely has changed the way we work personally at an individual level, but in team productivity, we still haven't seen those remarkable multiple productivity improvements, in the order of multiples, we have not seen that (yet).”

This is precisely why Zoho is staying clear of slapping a massive, power-hungry external LLM onto an existing platform as a quick feature fix. Instead, its AI deployment strategy is anchored around the core principle of “right-sizing” models based on the specific context of the task.

Currently, Zoho is using a highly coordinated matrix of 80-plus specialised machine learning models alongside small, medium, and large language models (all in-house) contextually embedded into the workflow to trigger the “next best action” for a user.

“Use a smaller model when you can, use a machine learning model when you can, and use the bigger models only when you do generative work, only when you do creative work,” Ramamoorthy advises. “So, if you look at a typical enterprise workflow in 10 steps, there are only one or two steps where you need that generative or creative capability. The rest of the things are going to be repeatable, retractable, and all of that.”

This disciplined approach to software modeling will now work in sync with the cost savings generated by the Nathu La hardware underneath. Because Zoho isn't bleeding capital to third-party public cloud providers or external LLM API vendors, it doesn’t have to treat intelligence features as a premium add-on for now. Its AI capabilities come pre-bundled inside the standard subscription tier and treated with the same architectural utility as an enterprise search tool, a mobile app, or a single sign-on protocol.

As Nathu La moves into active production, Zoho’s engineering leadership is intentionally playing the long game. While the system is built on Intel's latest 6th Generation Xeon processors in close collaboration with Intel India, the assembly, structural execution, and intellectual design are thoroughly indigenous. The project aligns directly with India's National Supercomputing Mission, its national open-source software mandates, and the overarching Atmanirbhar Bharat Abhiyan. Nathu La server

For the immediate future, Zoho is keeping its hardware deployments entirely internal and tightly focused on its India-based data centres, ensuring its engineering support frameworks mature safely before expanding the server infrastructure to its 150+ global hubs. The production pipeline is scaling up quickly. A few hundred server units are currently operating in limited deployment, with a clear roadmap to install 2,000 active Nathu La servers by the close of 2026.

When pressed on whether Zoho will eventually challenge global legacy hardware OEMs by selling these servers directly to external corporate buyers, Sadafale says, “Today we cannot comment on that because these global OEMs have a very strong supply chain support system. It's a massive thing. Designing an enterprise-class system is one thing, getting it working is one thing, getting it into production is one thing, and supporting it in the long term is another thing. We have just crossed the first step. So, there is a long way to go. Right now, it's a local thing. We'll do it locally. But our roadmap is strong. It's not like one product saying, ‘Oh yeah, we are done.’ No, we have a strong roadmap, and we are very well integrated with the semiconductor supplier.”

Ultimately, the creation and execution of Nathu La serves as a powerful reminder of the enduring value of human talent in a world increasingly fascinated by AI automation. It is, for all intents and purposes, a strong counterargument to prophesies about AI wiping out white-collar jobs made by the likes of Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei.

“Definitely, the way we create content, the way we do things has changed, but a lot still comes back to human skills, human empathy,” Ramamoorthy says. “Human capital is still the best thing that a company can have. It is irreplaceable by any LLM. Today it's difficult to build a server from scratch using an LLM. You need a Mangesh with years of experience and wisdom in building things. A lot of things that are undocumented reside in a (human) head, and you need that kind of leadership. And that leadership happens when you start nurturing people from the beginning. Things will come back to normalcy, but this is going to be a slightly new normal. You still can't work the same way you worked in 2022, but then we get to keep our jobs.”

- Ends

It is irreplaceable by any LLM. Today it's difficult to build a server from scratch using an LLM. You need a Mangesh with years of experience and wisdom in building things. A lot of things that are undocumented reside in a (human) head, and you need that kind of leadership. And that leadership happens when you start nurturing people from the beginning. Things will come back to normalcy, but this is going to be a slightly new normal. You still can't work the same way you worked in 2022, but then we get to keep our jobs.” - Ends
clean_text: From SaaS to silicon: How Zoho and a team of fresh engineering grads built an indigenous enterprise server For a nation whose tech narrative has been written almost entirely in lines of code, software giant Zoho is pulling off the ultimate flex. When Mangesh Sadafale, the Head of Hardware Development at Zoho, went to Sikkim on a family vacation a few years ago, he could not see, or rather he could not cross the iconic Nathu La pass due to bad weather. Years later, he would find himself staring down a different kind of impassable terrain. The road to designing an enterprise-grade, homegrown data centre server from scratch seemed as arduous. Turns out, calling it “Nathu La” was the easiest job. Read Full Story “I proposed, can we give it the Nathu La pass name? The team said yes,” Sadafale tells India Today Tech. “Then we decided, when ‘La’ and ‘Pass’ mean the same thing, why keep ‘Pass’ there? We will be Indian and keep whatever our local people use. We removed the ‘Pass’.” The rest, as they say, is history. In a country whose tech narrative has been almost exclusively written in lines of code, a software company may have just pulled off the hardest trick in deep-tech hardware. But the audacity doesn’t end there. The team that has designed the Nathu La server isn’t a room full of Silicon Valley veterans. It is a crew of fresh engineering graduates from the quiet, central city of Nagpur. Why Nathu La To be fair, Nathu La wasn’t built in a day. Research and development began five years ago when sanctions on chips and now, on advanced AI models like Claude Fable 5 and Mythos, were few and far between. In a sense, it was foresight. What’s wild is that a SaaS startup thought about it first. Not some heavyweight like TCS or Infosys. “Inference cost is now a line item and not a rounding error,” explains Ramprakash Ramamoorthy, Director of AI Research at Zoho. “Typically, if you look at a software company's balance sheet, the bigger expense is going to be people. The second thing is going to be operations like travel, office, and all of that. The third thing is your server cost which is your inference cost. Not just AI inference, all inference. But now it (inference cost) is moving up, and eventually, I think next year it will become the first big line item for a software company's expenses.” Ramprakash Ramamoorthy is the Director of AI Research at Zoho Corporation Over the past six months alone, the tech industry has watched global component pricing spike to unprecedented heights. Generic servers have seen their market prices surge by four times compared to December 2025, Ramamoorthy reveals. Securing these components, too, is an uphill task as demand is far exceeding the supply. Procurement delays can stretch into multiple quarters. Most big players are relying on public cloud companies or third-party Large Language Model (LLM) providers, handing over massive chunks of their revenue to keep the lights on. When those infrastructure costs go up, the surplus gets pushed downstream. It was clear from the start that Zoho would not pass the burden to its customers. advertisement “Renting (hardware) forever stops making sense because the competition is getting cutthroat, and then we are primarily building on top of the infrastructure that we have underneath,” says Ramamoorthy. "So, if compute becomes foundational, we should own it. We want to be a predictable and a boring company to do business with. That is one of the reasons we invest a lot in the foundation.” Underneath all the chaos and uncertainty, lurks another big issue, that of national significance. “If we look at the top three imports what India has done in the last couple of years, electronics and semiconductors stay at the third position, very close to gold with a narrow margin. Now, in compute electronics, for India, electronics is the third trade deficit, whereas if we compare it with China, electronics is the top number one contributor to their trade surplus,” Sadafale points out. In the last financial year alone, India imported more than $13 billion worth of complete compute systems. In stark contrast, during that exact same timeframe, China exported more than $150 billion of compute hardware. When Indian enterprises purchase servers today, the vast majority of those funds do not stay within the domestic ecosystem. Instead, the capital flows directly to the countries and multinational corporations that own the core intellectual property. advertisement “The country which owns the IP, owns the design, gets the bigger section of the payment, and the country which manufactures it gets the relatively smaller section of the payment,” Sadafale explains. “Now, when we look at it from an India perspective, the IP creation of critical infrastructure like server networks is very important. If we create IP, if we create a design, then only we will be able to sustain that cash flow or stop the outflow of money from India.” Sadafale cites the example of manufacturing, or rather, assembling of smartphones in India to make his point. “India doesn't have a solid mobile brand which owns the solid mobile IP and mobile design which can be manufactured in India and exported, so India is paying royalty to some other company outside, and the cash flow is going out of India. Deep tech knowledge cannot be licensed. It has to be homegrown. To maximise the value addition in India, the design has to be made in India,” he says candidly. advertisement Inside Nathu La When Zoho started its hardware initiative, it skipped over the crowded, hyper-competitive tech ecosystems of Bengaluru, Chennai, and Delhi. Instead, it established the hardware team in Nagpur, with the reasoning that it was located in "central" India. However, Zoho hit an immediate roadblock. In India, the specialised talent pool capable of executing advanced system design or Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) engineering is notoriously small, heavily concentrated in specific corporate corridors, and highly resistant to relocating to a Tier-2 city. “The only available talent pool was freshers in and around local colleges,” Sadafale says. “But the freshers, as everyone knows, they are more interested in software or AI. No one is interested in hardware. By default, hardware is hard. No one wants to work on a hard problem. And hardware is a hard problem. Over and above, in the last few years, AI is also creating trouble. It is spoiling the creativity of students. To counter this, Zoho engineered the SETU program (Student Engagement for Transformative Upskilling), stepping into local government engineering colleges to look for raw, unfiltered curiosity. advertisement “We don't care about CGPA or marks. We just care about curiosity and ability to learn,” Sadafale emphasises. “In SETU, we start interaction with students in the fifth semester itself. We give them a basic problem statement in hardware design. It can be mechanical, electronics, or firmware. As the students move from the sixth and seventh semester, the problem statements become more complicated. And at the end of the semester, based on how the students have performed, we offer them an internship in the eighth semester.” To date, the SETU program has trained over 300 students. It aligns closely with the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology's (MeitY) skill development initiatives for the Electronic System Design and Manufacturing (ESDM) sector. But more importantly, an astonishing 90 percent of the engineering team responsible for creating the Nathu La server platform is composed of these local college graduates who joined the firm over the last five years. Excluding Sadafale's own 25 years of industry expertise, the average professional experience across the entire Nagpur hardware office sits at just three to four years. Throwing early-career engineers directly into the deep end of advanced enterprise server development may sound like a recipe for disaster, but Sadafale believes their lack of institutional bias is a major competitive advantage. “Freshers can do magic because they don't know what is complex and what is simple,” he notes. “So, (when) you throw the problem at them, they will figure out the solution. You just have to protect them and help them in a way that it nurtures them, and it works out. It's great. It just works.” Nathu La is a living example of it. A high-performance enterprise server requires three distinct engineering areas to operate in perfect harmony: mechanical design for structural integrity and cooling, electronics design for high-speed signal distribution, and firmware to bridge the physical circuitry with the operating system. The Nagpur team systematically tackled all three. At its core, the Nathu La motherboard is built around the Open Compute 'M-DNO' form factor, a highly modular architecture that allows the system to be rapidly reconfigured for vastly different enterprise data centre workloads. Rather than deploying rigid, generic servers that run at subpar efficiency, Zoho can alter the underlying module configurations to precisely match the software application running on top of them. “Nathu La is a modular design which is workload-optimised,” Sadafale explains. “In Zoho Mail, we normally send text messages. Text is relatively small data information saved in storage to retrieve it when we want to see the mail again. Whereas in a Meeting, it is audio-video information, which is relatively much higher data. It has to be processed more because we are doing screen sharing, recording, background removal, and multiple people are changing their configuration. Meeting needs a different type of compute, a different type of server, whereas Mail needs a different type of compute and server.” Mangesh Sadafale, the Head of Hardware Development at Zoho By tailoring the server architecture to the specific data profile of the application, Nathu La can achieve an impressive 12 to 18 percent lower power consumption profile alongside a remarkable 20 to 30 percent reduction in total cost of ownership (TCO), Zoho claims. The platform boasts five distinct patents, the bigger pieces going into cooling. “Thermal is the number one concern today even if I take an air-cooled system like Nathu La,” says Sadafale. “The future systems will be liquid cooled. But in air-cooled also, our patents, the majority of them are for cooling. How efficiently you cool the systems, what are the small things, how the air flows, that is the actual patent. People will say, ‘What is the big deal?’ But that is a big deal.” Beyond the physical steel and circuitry lies the critical layer of firmware. Nathu La eliminates the opaque “black box” code supplied by traditional overseas hardware vendors, replacing it with a completely auditable, transparent “white box” software stack based on open-source standards like EDK2 for the BIOS and OpenBMC for the Baseboard Management Controller. The BMC acts as the operational nervous system of a modern data centre, allowing a single operator to monitor, diagnostic test, and maintain thousands of separate server stacks from a centralised console. By modifying open-source code for their specific needs, Zoho’s infrastructure engineers can know exactly what is happening inside every single data path. “We took open-source code and then modified it for our needs,” Sadafale explains. “So, we know each and every line of the code that has gone in. We know what signal is going here and there. There is hardware security we have added in so that even if someone takes the server outside (the country) and tries to hack something, that will not happen.” Beyond Nathu La The rollout of the Nathu La server platform coincides with a broader conversation regarding the sustainable integration of AI within the enterprise ecosystem. We know – and we have seen – that AI tools like Claude Code can upend the SaaS industry. Whether it can bring the SaaSpocalypse, that remains debatable. But as the initial wave of excitement settles, the market is beginning to demand tangible proof of value, that which OpenAI, Anthropic and even Google, have promised. “The whole craze for all these memory prices going crazy, all of this is due to the AI mad rush,” Ramamoorthy notes. “But the good thing is I see some of it slowing down, at least in the last couple of weeks. We're hearing a lot more conversations about return on token investment – ‘I spent so much, what is the tangible output that I have received?’” Across its corporate legal workflows, sales optimisation pipelines, and customer support channels, Zoho is seeing a steady, incremental 20 to 25 percent improvement in overall employee productivity using AI. “If a human support engineer is solving 10 tickets a day, they will be able to solve 12 tickets a day,” Ramamoorthy states plainly. “And that is the incremental benefit that we have seen so far. The reality is coming back to the table. I hope that contains the mad rush of throwing crazy compute at internet-scale data. It (AI) definitely has changed the way we work personally at an individual level, but in team productivity, we still haven't seen those remarkable multiple productivity improvements, in the order of multiples, we have not seen that (yet).” This is precisely why Zoho is staying clear of slapping a massive, power-hungry external LLM onto an existing platform as a quick feature fix. Instead, its AI deployment strategy is anchored around the core principle of “right-sizing” models based on the specific context of the task. Currently, Zoho is using a highly coordinated matrix of 80-plus specialised machine learning models alongside small, medium, and large language models (all in-house) contextually embedded into the workflow to trigger the “next best action” for a user. “Use a smaller model when you can, use a machine learning model when you can, and use the bigger models only when you do generative work, only when you do creative work,” Ramamoorthy advises. “So, if you look at a typical enterprise workflow in 10 steps, there are only one or two steps where you need that generative or creative capability. The rest of the things are going to be repeatable, retractable, and all of that.” This disciplined approach to software modeling will now work in sync with the cost savings generated by the Nathu La hardware underneath. Because Zoho isn't bleeding capital to third-party public cloud providers or external LLM API vendors, it doesn’t have to treat intelligence features as a premium add-on for now. Its AI capabilities come pre-bundled inside the standard subscription tier and treated with the same architectural utility as an enterprise search tool, a mobile app, or a single sign-on protocol. As Nathu La moves into active production, Zoho’s engineering leadership is intentionally playing the long game. While the system is built on Intel's latest 6th Generation Xeon processors in close collaboration with Intel India, the assembly, structural execution, and intellectual design are thoroughly indigenous. The project aligns directly with India's National Supercomputing Mission, its national open-source software mandates, and the overarching Atmanirbhar Bharat Abhiyan. Nathu La server For the immediate future, Zoho is keeping its hardware deployments entirely internal and tightly focused on its India-based data centres, ensuring its engineering support frameworks mature safely before expanding the server infrastructure to its 150+ global hubs. The production pipeline is scaling up quickly. A few hundred server units are currently operating in limited deployment, with a clear roadmap to install 2,000 active Nathu La servers by the close of 2026. When pressed on whether Zoho will eventually challenge global legacy hardware OEMs by selling these servers directly to external corporate buyers, Sadafale says, “Today we cannot comment on that because these global OEMs have a very strong supply chain support system. It's a massive thing. Designing an enterprise-class system is one thing, getting it working is one thing, getting it into production is one thing, and supporting it in the long term is another thing. We have just crossed the first step. So, there is a long way to go. Right now, it's a local thing. We'll do it locally. But our roadmap is strong. It's not like one product saying, ‘Oh yeah, we are done.’ No, we have a strong roadmap, and we are very well integrated with the semiconductor supplier.” Ultimately, the creation and execution of Nathu La serves as a powerful reminder of the enduring value of human talent in a world increasingly fascinated by AI automation. It is, for all intents and purposes, a strong counterargument to prophesies about AI wiping out white-collar jobs made by the likes of Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei. “Definitely, the way we create content, the way we do things has changed, but a lot still comes back to human skills, human empathy,” Ramamoorthy says. “Human capital is still the best thing that a company can have. It is irreplaceable by any LLM. Today it's difficult to build a server from scratch using an LLM. You need a Mangesh with years of experience and wisdom in building things. A lot of things that are undocumented reside in a (human) head, and you need that kind of leadership. And that leadership happens when you start nurturing people from the beginning. Things will come back to normalcy, but this is going to be a slightly new normal. You still can't work the same way you worked in 2022, but then we get to keep our jobs.” - Ends

title: Intelegain Marks 26 Years of Transforming Enterprises Through Technology
content:
Mumbai, India — June 2026

Twenty-six years is a long time in any industry. In enterprise technology, it’s a lifetime. When Intelegain started out, businesses were still figuring out the internet. Desktop software ruled the office. Dial-up connections were the norm. The world enterprises operate in today looks nothing like that. What has stayed consistent, through every shift in platform, paradigm, and expectation, is the company itself.

Founded in 2000, Intelegain began as a product engineering firm helping businesses make sense of the emerging digital world. Today, it operates as a full-spectrum technology partner, with offices spanning India, the USA, the UK, Singapore, the UAE, and Australia, delivering everything from enterprise ERP implementations and AI-powered product development to cloud infrastructure, data analytics, and marketing automation. The journey from then to now isn’t a straight line. It’s the result of deliberate evolution, built on long client relationships and a consistent refusal to stand still.

Building What Businesses Actually Need

Ask any enterprise technology leader what they want from a partner, and the answer is rarely about features or frameworks. It’s about someone who understands their business well enough to cut through complexity – and delivers. That’s the space Intelegain has occupied across two and a half decades.

Today, the company’s capabilities span several interconnected domains. Microsoft Dynamics 365 sits at the core of much of their enterprise work, Business Central ERP, Sales CRM, and Marketing Automation, helping mid-to-large organizations move away from fragmented, manual systems toward something that actually scales. For companies still running legacy platforms like NAV, Tally, QuickBooks, or Sage, Intelegain manages the migration end-to-end, removing the anxiety that typically accompanies those transitions.

On the product and application side, the team designs and builds high-performance web and mobile applications, custom solutions shaped around specific operational needs, not off-the-shelf compromises. Their UI/UX practice, developed under the UXSense umbrella, brings the same rigour to user experience that enterprise companies typically reserve for infrastructure.

Cloud engineering, primarily on Azure, and Power BI-led data analytics round out the offering, giving clients the infrastructure to move fast and the visibility to make smarter decisions. And more recently, Intelegain’s Agentic AI services and Microsoft 365 Copilot implementations have positioned the company squarely in the middle of where enterprise technology is heading next.

From Startups to Global Brands

The client roster tells a story of breadth and trust. Heineken partnered with Intelegain to build a bilingual distributor portal integrated with Business Central – one now used by over 1,000 sub-distributors across markets. Allcargo Logistics worked with the team to digitize end-to-end cargo operations across India. Octave Holdings deployed an Agentic AI chatbot that now handles over 85% of investor interactions, significantly improving onboarding speed and conversion. Wonderchef, Pfizer, SBI, Sharekhan, Clariness, Polyflor – across manufacturing, logistics, BFSI, pharma, wholesale, retail, and hospitality. Here, the common thread is organizations that need technology to actually work, not just look good on a slide.

That trust has also earned recognition from third parties. Intelegain has received Microsoft ecosystem recognition, including the APAC and Global ISV P2P Awards. This reflects the depth of the company’s Microsoft practice, not just its breadth. The CRN Excellence Award for Enterprise Apps and the SME Channels Award for IT as a Service further validate what clients already know from experience.

A People Business, Not Just a Technology Business

Milestones like this one invite reflection, and the leadership team is quick to point to the people, both internal and external, who made the last 26 years possible. Building great technology is table stakes. Keeping clients through economic downturns, technology inflection points, and industry disruptions requires something harder to define: reliability, responsiveness, and a genuine investment in the client’s outcomes over the long term.

That orientation shapes how Intelegain is approaching the next chapter. Agentic AI is not a trend to experiment with cautiously; instead, it’s a fundamental shift in how enterprises can automate decision-making, simplify complex operations, and reimagine customer-facing experiences. The company is already deep in implementations, working with clients across financial services, logistics, and manufacturing to move beyond chatbots toward intelligent systems that take action.

Looking Ahead

Twenty-six years in, Intelegain isn’t marking an anniversary so much as a vantage point. The tools have changed dramatically, from client-server applications to cloud-native platforms, from rule-based automation to AI agents that reason and act, but the core challenge remains the same, that is: helping enterprises adapt without losing momentum.

The team building those solutions today is larger, more specialized, and more globally distributed than at any point in the company’s history. And the problems they’re being asked to solve are genuinely harder, with more complex integrations, higher stakes, tighter timelines, and clients with zero tolerance for vague timelines or overengineered solutions.

That’s exactly the kind of challenge Intelegain was built for.

About Intelegain

Intelegain is a global IT consulting and product engineering company specializing in enterprise software development, Microsoft Dynamics 365, cloud services, AI/ML, and data analytics. With offices in India, the USA, the UK, Singapore, the UAE, and Australia, the company has served clients across manufacturing, logistics, BFSI, pharmaceuticals, retail, and hospitality for over 26 years.

Website: www.intelegain.com

Email: contactus@intelegain.com

title: To Go Fast and Far: Why Africa's Businesses Are Rebuilding Technology in 2026
content:
CAPE TOWN, South Africa, June 13, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- This is exactly why Junkies Coder an Africa-based software engineering company has launched its Agentic AI & Enterprise Modernisation practice, a dedicated unit built to help African enterprises deploy AI agents reliably by first modernising the legacy systems, cloud infrastructure, and delivery pipelines those agents depend on.

There is an old African saying: 'If you want to go fast, go alone; if you want to go far, go together.'

Junkies Coder Enterprise Business for AI Transformation and Application Modernization

For decades, Africa's largest banks, telecom companies, and retailers were forced to choose between speed and scale. In 2026, enterprises realize they need both and the way they build technology is changing because of it.

The pressure is measurable. South Africa's largest banks increased IT spending by up to 32% in 2025 as they accelerated core system modernization and AI adoption.

Yet many still struggle with legacy platforms that cannot keep pace with modern customer expectations: instant onboarding, real-time payments, and seamless mobile experiences.

Meanwhile, fintech companies and challenger banks are gaining ground. They are not necessarily bigger, but they run on cloud-native, modular platforms that launch new products in weeks instead of years.

This is the defining challenge for African enterprises in 2026: the foundations are finally available for hyperscale data centres in Johannesburg and Cape Town and billions committed to cloud but the ability to execute remains the real gap.

Two forces are slowing modernization:

Digital talent shortage. South Africa continues to face shortages in critical digital and engineering skills, amplified by migration. Across Africa, nine in ten organizations report significant impact from a lack of AI skills. Compliance complexity. Regulations such as POPIA are raising accountability. Data residency alone does not guarantee compliance businesses remain responsible for how applications are built, managed, and secured.

The result is clear: African enterprises have the capital and urgency to modernise, but lack the talent, approach, and execution framework to transform safely.

This is precisely the gap Junkies Coder South Africa closes. Instead of choosing between a costly internal team and a slow, oversized integrator, the company partners as a co-founding-style technology team bringing founder empathy, MVP-first delivery, and one integrated discipline across architecture, cloud, DevOps, and compliance. The company helps enterprises modernise in the right sequence: fixing high-impact workflows first, shipping continuously, and designing data residency and privacy from day one.

For Africa's brands, the lesson of 2026 is clear. Modernisation will not wait for the talent market to heal. The enterprises that win this decade will find a partner that lets them go fast and far — together.

Junkies Coder is an AI-driven enterprise software and mobile app development company in South Africa partnering with Africa's founders and giants to modernise architecture, build modern DevOps pipelines, and engineer compliance by design.

Website - https://www.junkiescoder.com/

title: Google warns that ShinyHunters group is exploiting Oracle flaw to target education sector
content:
Google warns that ShinyHunters group is exploiting Oracle flaw to target education sector

Google Threat Intelligence Group reportedly announced on Thursday that it had identified an extortion campaign targeting Oracle’s enterprise software. The company explicitly attributed the attack to the hacking group ShinyHunters.

Google clarified that it has already notified more than 100 organizations whose IP addresses were linked to potentially exposed endpoints.

Advertisement

It has been observed that most of the IP addresses were based in the US, and 68% belonged to the higher edition center.

According to researchers, the attackers hosted customized MeshCentral agents' disguised as legitimate endpoints which were then used to run administrative queries.

The hackers took full advantage of a “zero-day” flaw, which implies that there was no patch available at the time of the attack. It is worth mentioning that this malicious activity occurred before Oracle issued a major security advisory on June 10.

Earlier ShinyHunters attack also shows weaponization of education continuity, holding the academic progress of millions hostage.

Mandiant and GTIG being the two core pillars of Google Threat Intelligence said: “The attacker staging environments hosted customized MeshCentral agents masquerading as legitimate cloud endpoints, which they used to run administrative command queries and deploy a custom lateral movement and defacement script.”

It further resulted in a data breach, and thousands of students have been unable to access their major education materials.

ShinyHunters is a hacking group with a history of targeting prominent companies. Most importantly, the group attempted to strike a deal with Instructure, after stealing teacher and student data.

title: Enterprise Software Development Partner Selection Guide
content:
Digitalizing core operations in mid-sized and large organizations is a multifaceted challenge where the stakes are incredibly high. Outdated legacy systems, rigid software architectures, and the friction of integrating fragmented data from multiple sources can heavily bottleneck business growth and lead to severe financial losses. When off-the-shelf software limits your performance, custom enterprise applications become the only viable path to building a lasting competitive advantage.

Choosing a software development company capable of executing such a complex project cannot be left to chance. This decision must be grounded in a pragmatic verification of the provider’s actual competencies.

Here are the key pillars to guide your evaluation and ensure a solid return on investment:

1. Understanding the Business, Not Just Delivering Features

The most successful digital projects start with a deep understanding of your business processes, not a blind transcription of a technical specification. An experienced technology partner does not act as a mere vendor—they serve as strategic advisors who are not afraid to challenge conventional assumptions.

A critical green flag at the start of a partnership is a structured Discovery and Product Analysis phase. Through dedicated mapping workshops, user interviews, and strategic scoping sessions, the provider aligns technical feasibility with your actual business goals and budget constraints. Look for a company that genuinely wants to understand how your organization operates.

2. Deep Engineering Competence in Legacy Modernization

Enterprises rarely build digital products in a vacuum. New software almost always has to seamlessly integrate with existing infrastructure or completely replace it without disrupting day-to-day business continuity.

It is vital to verify whether your potential technology partner has a proven track record in legacy software modernization. Whether your project demands a swift cloud migration, an architectural refactoring, or a complete rewrite from the ground up, the engineering team must demonstrate strict technical rigor and explicit risk management.

Proven Industry Track Record: For a practical reference of a knowledgeable practitioner voice delivering enterprise-level results, you can explore the dedicated custom development capabilities at Merixstudio’s Enterprise Software Development Services: https://www.merixstudio.com/services/enterprise-software-development. Backed by over 25 years of market experience, their cross-functional teams specialize in navigating high-stakes engineering environments.

3. A User-Centric Approach in Data-Rich Environments

Enterprise systems process massive volumes of information. However, data-driven technology only yields business value if your employees can interact with it efficiently and confidently.

Confusing a powerful technical interface with a crude or complex user experience is a costly mistake. A reliable development partner prioritizes Product Design and robust UX/UI research. Transforming complex datasets into clear, visualization-rich dashboards drastically lowers the cognitive load on staff, slashes onboarding and training times, and minimizes the risk of expensive human errors.

4. Security and Global Standards as a Trust Foundation

In the enterprise segment, compromises on data protection and intellectual property are non-negotiable. You must choose software partners whose development culture, internal policies, and source code handling are externally certified.

The baseline requirement for any serious enterprise project is compliance with international standards:

ISO 9001: Guarantees a mature, repeatable quality management system across the entire software development lifecycle.

ISO 27001: Ensures that client data, source code, and deployment environments are strictly protected under rigorous information security protocols.

5. Responsible AI Integration and Scalability

Modern software engineering is evolving rapidly due to generative AI capabilities. When evaluating developers, ask directly about how they manage innovation responsibly.

Mature engineering companies incorporate AI assistants into their delivery pipelines in a structured, audited manner. A professional partner enforces a strict zero-data retention policy, utilizing enterprise-grade, centrally managed tools to ensure your proprietary source code is never used to train public models. When implemented responsibly, AI optimization accelerates repetitive development tasks, automated testing, and code refactoring by 10% to 25%, directly speeding up your product’s Time-to-Market without compromising security.

Summary: Looking Beyond Hourly Rates

Choosing an enterprise software development partner is a long-term strategic decision. Rather than evaluating providers solely on hourly rates, prioritize a pragmatic, transparent collaborator with established market maturity. The ultimate differentiators of a top-tier partner are certified quality standards, robust engineering depth, and a business-aware advisory approach that focuses on the value delivered, not just the backlog.

title: TCS partners with Anthropic to deploy Claude across enterprise operations at scale
content:
Live Events



as a Reliable and Trusted News Source Addas a Reliable and Trusted News Source Add Now!

Not long ago, a large software project would typically require teams of developers, analysts, and project managers to work together over several months. Today, advances in artificial intelligence are beginning to reshape that process, enabling organisations to automate routine tasks, accelerate development cycles, and enhance decision-making at scale.This shift is prompting businesses to rethink how work is organised and how technology creates value. The conversation around AI has moved beyond experimentation and pilot projects to a more fundamental question: How can organisations embed AI into the core of their operations?That is what makes the partnership between Tata Consultancy Services TCS ) and Anthropic significant.By becoming one of Anthropic’s key global partners, TCS is gaining access to advanced AI capabilities through Claude, Anthropic’s family of large language models . But this is not simply about adopting a new technology platform. It is about building an AI-first enterprise model that could influence how businesses operate in the years ahead.The collaboration will see TCS deploy AI across software engineering, finance, legal, marketing, customer service, and business operations. Thousands of employees are expected to work alongside AI systems, using them not just as assistants but as active participants in day-to-day workflows.This reflects a broader shift taking place across industries. AI is moving beyond content generation and automation to become what many describe as an “agentic” technology. Unlike traditional software that waits for instructions, AI agents can analyse information, make recommendations, execute tasks, and manage multi-step processes with limited human intervention.For enterprises, this opens the door to a fundamentally different operating model. Software development can be accelerated by AI-generated code. Customer queries can be resolved through intelligent agents. Internal workflows can be managed more efficiently, reducing manual effort and improving decision-making speed.The implications extend far beyond productivity gains. As AI becomes integrated into core business processes, organisations will increasingly compete on how effectively they deploy and scale intelligent systems. Access to powerful AI models alone will not be enough; success will depend on how well companies combine technology with domain expertise, governance, and human oversight.For TCS, the partnership represents an opportunity to position itself at the centre of this transformation. As enterprises worldwide look for guidance on adopting AI responsibly and at scale, technology service providers will play a critical role in translating AI’s potential into measurable business outcomes.More broadly, the partnership highlights where the AI industry is heading next. The first wave was about building powerful models. The second wave was about experimentation. The next phase will focus on large-scale adoption, in which AI becomes embedded across every layer of an organisation.In that sense, the TCS-Anthropic collaboration is not just another technology announcement. It is a glimpse into a future where AI is no longer a tool businesses use occasionally, but a capability woven into how they operate, innovate, and grow.
description: TCS and Anthropic have partnered to deploy Claude across enterprise operations, from software engineering to customer service, signalling a decisive shift from AI experimentation to large-scale adoption. As AI evolves into an agentic technology capable of executing complex, multi-step tasks, businesses are no longer asking whether to adopt AI, but how to embed it at the core of how they operate. For TCS, this collaboration is a strategic move to lead that transformation on behalf of enterprises worldwide.
